To create a C program for the Raspberry Pi Zero W that regulates a PWM-controlled 5V cooling fan based on temperature readings from a MAX31855 thermocouple, we'll need to perform the following steps:

1. Initialize the hardware.
2. Read temperature data from the MAX31855 sensor via SPI.
3. Control the fan speed using PWM on GPIO18.
4. Log the temperature and fan speed to a file named `fan_log.txt`.
5. Handle any SPI errors gracefully.

We will use the `wiringPi` library for GPIO and SPI operations. Ensure you have `wiringPi` installed on your Raspberry Pi Zero W before proceeding. You can install it using the following commands:

```sh
sudo apt-get update
git clone git://git.drogon.net/wiringPi
cd wiringPi
./build
```

Here is the complete C program:

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <fcntl.h>
#include <wiringPi.h>
#include <wiringPiSPI.h>

#define MAX31855_CS_PIN 0
#define FAN_PIN 18

#define MAX31855_REG_CONFIG 0x01
#define MAX31855_REG_RTDMSB 0x02
#define MAX31855_REG_RTDLSB 0x03
#define MAX31855_REG_CONFIG_DEFAULT 0x86 // Continuous conversion mode

int spi_fd;

void init_spi() {
    if (wiringPiSPISetup(MAX31855_CS_PIN, 1000000) < 0) { // 1 MHz clock speed
        perror("Failed to setup SPI");
        exit(EXIT_FAILURE);
    }
}

float read_temperature() {
    unsigned char rx[3];
    unsigned char tx[1] = {MAX31855_REG_RTDMSB};
    int result;

    if ((result = wiringPiSPIDataRW(MAX31855_CS_PIN, tx, 1)) != 1) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Failed to read RTDMSB register: %d\n", result);
        return -1;
    }

    if ((result = wiringPiSPIDataRW(MAX31855_CS_PIN, tx, 3)) != 3) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Failed to read RTDLB register: %d\n", result);
        return -1;
    }

    rx[0] = tx[MAX31855_REG_RTDMSB];
    rx[1] = tx[MAX31855_REG_RTDLSB];

    float raw_temp = (rx[0] << 8) | rx[1];

    return raw_temp / 340.0 + 50.0; // Conversion formula for MAX31855
}

void set_fan_speed(float speed) {
    int duty_cycle = speed * 100;
    wiringPiPwmWrite(FAN_PIN, duty_cycle);
}

int main(void) {
    if (wiringPiSetup() == -1) {
        printf("wiringPi setup failed.\n");
        return 1;
    }

    init_spi();

    wiringPiSPISetupMode(spi_fd, 1000000, 0); // 1 MHz clock speed, MSBFIRST

    FILE *log_file = fopen("fan_log.txt", "a+");
    if (!log_file) {
        perror("Failed to open log file");
        return 1;
    }

    while (1) {
        float temp = read_temperature();
        if (temp >= 0) {
            printf("Current temperature: %.2f C\n", temp);
            set_fan_speed(temp > 70 ? 1.0 : 0.0); // Simple control logic
            fprintf(log_file, "Time: %s, Temperature: %.2f C, Fan Speed: %.2f%%\n", ctime(&time(NULL)), temp, set_fan_speed(temp > 70 ? 1.0 : 0.0));
        } else {
            printf("Error reading temperature.\n");
        }

        sleep(1); // Poll every second
    }

    fclose(log_file);
    return 0;
}
```

3. **Fan Speed Control**: The `set_fan_speed()` function sets the fan speed as a percentage of full speed.
4. **Main Loop**: The `main()` function continuously reads the temperature, controls the fan speed based on a simple threshold, and logs the data to `fan_log.txt`.

#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <stdint.h>
#include <stdbool.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <time.h>
#include <signal.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <wiringPi.h>
#include <wiringPiSPI.h>

// Define constants
#define FAN_GPIO 18         // GPIO pin for PWM control
#define SPI_CHANNEL 0       // SPI channel to use
#define SPI_SPEED 1000000   // SPI clock speed (1 MHz)
#define NUM_READS 5        // Number of SPI reads to average
#define PWM_RANGE 100       // PWM range (0-100)
#define LOG_FILE "fan_log.txt"

// Global variables
FILE *log_file = NULL;
bool running = true;

// Function prototypes
float read_max31855_temperature();
int calculate_fan_speed(float temperature);
void log_data(float temperature, int fan_speed);
void signal_handler(int signal);

// Structure to hold configuration data
typedef struct {
    int spi_channel;
    int spi_speed;
    int pwm_gpio;
    float temp_threshold;
} config_t;


// Function to read temperature from MAX31855 with averaging
float read_max31855_temperature() {
    unsigned char buffer[NUM_READS];
    int i, j;
    float sum = 0.0f;

    for (i = 0; i < NUM_READS; i++) {
        if (wiringPiSPIDataRW(SPI_CHANNEL, buffer + i * 4, 4) == -1) {
            fprintf(stderr, "SPI read failed at attempt %d\n", i + 1);
            return -273.15f; // Return absolute zero on failure
        }
    }

    for (i = 0; i < NUM_READS; i++) {
        int32_t raw_data = (buffer[i * 4] << 24) | (buffer[i * 4 + 1] << 16) | (buffer[i * 4 + 2] << 8) | buffer[i * 4 + 3];

        // Check for fault conditions
        if (raw_data & 0x00000004) {
            fprintf(stderr, "Fault detected: Open circuit or short to GND\n");
            return -273.15f; // Return absolute zero on fault
        } else if (raw_data & 0x00000002) {
            fprintf(stderr, "Fault detected: Short to VCC\n");
            return -273.15f;
        } else if (raw_data & 0x00000001) {
            fprintf(stderr, "Fault detected: Thermocouple disconnected\n");
            return -273.15f;
        }

        // Extract thermocouple temperature (bits 18-31, signed)
        int16_t thermocouple_temp = (raw_data >> 18) & 0x3FFF;
        float temp = thermocouple_temp * 0.25f;

        sum += temp;
    }

    return sum / NUM_READS;
}

// Function to calculate fan speed based on temperature
int calculate_fan_speed(float temperature) {
    int speed = 0;

    if (temperature > 40.0f) {
        speed = 100; // Full speed
    } else if (temperature > 30.0f) {
        speed = (int)(((temperature - 30.0f) / (40.0f - 30.0f)) * 100.0f);
    } else if (temperature > 20.0f) {
        speed = (int)(((temperature - 20.0f) / (30.0f - 20.0f)) * 50.0f);
    } else {
        speed = 0; // Off
    }

    return speed;
}

// Function to log data to file
void log_data(float temperature, int fan_speed) {
    time_t t = time(NULL);
    struct tm tm = *localtime(&t);

    fprintf(log_file, "%d-%02d-%02d %02d:%02d:%02d, Temperature: %.2f C, Fan Speed: %d%%\n",
            tm.tm_year + 1900, tm.tm_mon + 1, tm.tm_mday, tm.tm_hour, tm.tm_min, tm.tm_sec,
            temperature, fan_speed);
    fflush(log_file); // Ensure data is written immediately
}

// Signal handler function
void signal_handler(int signal) {
    printf("Caught signal %d, exiting gracefully...\n", signal);
    running = false;
}

int main() {
    // Initialize WiringPi
    if (wiringPiSetupGpio() == -1) {
        fprintf(stderr, "WiringPi initialization failed!\n");
        return 1;
    }

    // Initialize SPI
    if (wiringPiSPISetup(SPI_CHANNEL, SPI_SPEED) == -1) {
        fprintf(stderr, "SPI setup failed!\n");
        return 1;
    }

    // Set PWM mode and range
    pinMode(FAN_GPIO, PWM_OUTPUT);
    pwmSetMode(PWM_MODE_MS);
    pwmSetRange(PWM_RANGE);

    // Open log file
    log_file = fopen(LOG_FILE, "a");
    if (log_file == NULL) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Failed to open log file!\n");
        return 1;
    }

    // Register signal handler
    signal(SIGINT, signal_handler);

    // Main loop
    while (running) {
        float temperature = read_max31855_temperature();
        if (temperature != -273.15f && temperature != -273.16f) { // Handle potential error returns
            int fan_speed = calculate_fan_speed(temperature);
            pwmWrite(FAN_GPIO, fan_speed);
            log_data(temperature, fan_speed);
        } else {
            fprintf(stderr, "Error reading temperature, skipping logging.\n");
        }

        delay(1000); // Wait 1 second
    }

    // Cleanup
    pwmWrite(FAN_GPIO, 0); // Turn off fan
    fclose(log_file);

    return 0;
}<|im_end|>

#include <stdio.h>

#include <string.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <sys/time.h>
#include <wiringPi.h>

// Constants
#define SENSOR_PIN 0    // Analog pin connected to the sensor
#define LOG_FILE "airflow_log.txt"
#define THRESHOLD_VOLTAGE 0.6 // Threshold voltage in volts (adjust based on your sensor)

// Function prototypes
int getVoltage(int pin);
void logData(double voltage, char* timestamp);
void setupGPIO();

int main() {
    int pin = SENSOR_PIN;
    double voltage;
    struct timeval tv;

    // Initialize wiringPi
    if (wiringPiSetup() == -1) {
        printf("wiringPi setup failed!\n");
        return 1;
    }

    setupGPIO();

    while (1) {
        // Get current time
        gettimeofday(&tv, NULL);
        // Read the voltage from the sensor
        voltage = getVoltage(pin);

        // Log the data
        logData(voltage, ctime((time_t*)&tv.tv_sec));

        // Check if the voltage is below the threshold
        if (voltage < THRESHOLD_VOLTAGE) {
            printf("Airflow is low: %f V\n", voltage);
            // Trigger an alarm (e.g., toggle a LED)
            pinMode(18, OUTPUT); // Assuming GPIO 18 is connected to an LED
            digitalWrite(18, HIGH);
            sleep(1); // Stay on for 1 second
            digitalWrite(18, LOW);
        }

        // Sleep for a short period before the next reading
        sleep(1);
    }

    return 0;
}

// Function to read the voltage from the ADC pin
int getVoltage(int pin) {
    int rawValue;
    float voltage;

    // Read the raw value from the ADC
    rawValue = analogRead(pin);
    // Convert raw value to voltage
    voltage = rawValue * (5.0 / 1023.0);

    return voltage;
}

// Function to log data to a file
void logData(double voltage, char* timestamp) {
    FILE *file;
    char logLine[100];

    // Open the log file
    file = fopen(LOG_FILE, "a");
    if (!file) {
        perror("Failed to open log file");
        exit(EXIT_FAILURE);
    }

    // Format the log line
    snprintf(logLine, sizeof(logLine), "%sVoltage: %.3f V\n", timestamp, voltage);

    // Write to the log file
    fprintf(file, logLine);
    fclose(file);
}

// Function to initialize GPIO
void setupGPIO() {
    // Set up GPIO for the LED (assuming GPIO 18 is used)
    pinMode(18, OUTPUT);
}

#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <pigpio.h>
#include <time.h>
#include <signal.h>

// Define constants
#define SPI_CHANNEL 0 // CE0
#define ADC_CHANNEL 0 // MCP3008 Channel 0
#define ALARM_GPIO 17 // GPIO pin for alarm
#define AIRFLOW_THRESHOLD 10.0 // Airflow threshold in SLPM
#define LOG_FILE "airflow_log.txt"

// Global variables
FILE *log_file = NULL;
int spi_handle;

// Function prototypes
float read_airflow();
void trigger_alarm();
void log_data(float voltage);
void signal_handler(int signal);

// Function to read airflow from the sensor
float read_airflow() {
    int count;
    unsigned char spi_tx_buf[3];
    unsigned char spi_rx_buf[3];

    // MCP3008 single-ended read command: start bit, single/diff bit, channel bits, don't care bit
    spi_tx_buf[0] = 0x01; // Start bit
    spi_tx_buf[1] = (0x08 | ADC_CHANNEL) << 4; // Single-ended, channel selection
    spi_tx_buf[2] = 0x00; // Don't care

    count = spiXfer(spi_handle, spi_tx_buf, spi_rx_buf, 3);

    if (count != 3) {
        fprintf(stderr, "SPI transfer error: %d\n", count);
        return -1.0;
    }

    // Extract the ADC value (10 bits)
    int adc_value = ((spi_rx_buf[1] & 0x03) << 8) + spi_rx_buf[2];

    // Convert ADC value to voltage (assuming 3.3V reference)
    float voltage = ((float)adc_value / 1023.0) * 3.3;

    return voltage;
}

// Function to trigger the alarm
void trigger_alarm() {
    gpioWrite(ALARM_GPIO, 1); // Set GPIO high
    printf("Alarm triggered!\n");
}

// Function to log data to file
void log_data(float voltage) {
    time_t current_time;
    struct tm *time_info;
    char timestamp[20];

    time(&current_time);
    time_info = localtime(&current_time);

    strftime(timestamp, sizeof(timestamp), "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S", time_info);

    if (log_file != NULL) {
        fprintf(log_file, "%s, %.2f V\n", timestamp, voltage);
        fflush(log_file); // Ensure data is written immediately
    } else {
        fprintf(stderr, "Error: Log file is not open.\n");
    }
}

// Signal handler for graceful shutdown
void signal_handler(int signal) {
    printf("Caught signal %d, exiting gracefully...\n", signal);

    if (log_file != NULL) {
        fclose(log_file);
    }

    if (spi_handle >= 0) {
        spiClose(spi_handle);
    }

    gpioTerminate();

    exit(0);
}

int main() {
    // Initialize pigpio
    if (gpioInitialise() < 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "pigpio initialisation failed\n");
        return 1;
    }

    // Open log file
    log_file = fopen(LOG_FILE, "a");
    if (log_file == NULL) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Error opening log file!\n");
        gpioTerminate();
        return 1;
    }

    // Configure alarm GPIO as output
    gpioSetMode(ALARM_GPIO, PI_OUTPUT);

    // Open SPI channel
    spi_handle = spiOpen(SPI_CHANNEL, 1000000, 0); // Channel 0, 1MHz, mode 0
    if (spi_handle < 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "SPI open failed\n");
        fclose(log_file);
        gpioTerminate();
        return 1;
    }

    // Set up signal handler for Ctrl+C
    signal(SIGINT, signal_handler);

    printf("Airflow monitoring started. Press Ctrl+C to exit.\n");

    while (1) {
        float voltage = read_airflow();

        if (voltage < 0.0) {
            fprintf(stderr, "Error reading airflow, retrying...\n");
            gpioDelay(1000); // Wait 1 second before retrying
            continue;
        }

        // Calculate airflow (example calculation, adjust based on sensor datasheet)
        float airflow = voltage * 20.0; // Example conversion

        printf("Airflow: %.2f SLPM, Voltage: %.2f V\n", airflow, voltage);

        log_data(voltage);

        if (airflow < AIRFLOW_THRESHOLD) {
            trigger_alarm();
        }

        gpioDelay(1000); // Read every 1 second
    }

    return 0; // This line will never be reached due to the infinite loop, but it's good practice to include it.

The program should continuously monitor airflow levels, log voltage values along with timestamps to a file named airflow_log.txt, and activate an alarm on a designated GPIO pin if the voltage falls below a set threshold, indicating low airflow conditions.
prompt:
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi 3 Model B+ to interface with a Honeywell AWM5300V airflow sensor (analog voltage output) using the wiringPi library. Implement a functional programming style, focusing on modular functions for each task. Specifically, use `wiringPiSetup()` to initialize wiringPi, `analogRead()` to read the sensor value (assuming it's connected to a MCP3008 ADC via SPI – specify SPI channel 0, CE0 pin 8, and the sensor is connected to ADC channel 0), and `digitalWrite()` to control an alarm LED connected to GPIO pin 1 (wiringPi pin numbering).

The program should log airflow readings to "airflow_log.txt" with a timestamp (using `time()` and `strftime()`) and the corresponding voltage value (calculated from the ADC reading). Define a function `float calculateVoltage(int adcValue)` to convert the ADC reading (0-1023) to a voltage (0-3.3V, assuming the ADC reference voltage is 3.3V).

Implement an airflow threshold alarm. Define a constant `float AIRFLOW_THRESHOLD` (e.g., 1.0 volts). If the calculated voltage drops below this threshold, trigger an alarm by setting the alarm LED (GPIO pin 1) HIGH for 2 seconds using `delay(2000)`, then setting it LOW.

The program should run continuously, reading the sensor every 500 milliseconds using `delay(500)`. Include proper error handling for file operations (using `fopen()`, `fprintf()`, `fclose()`) and wiringPi initialization.

Implement a standalone approach, suitable for motion-triggered surveillance, where low airflow could indicate a lack of activity.

Include signal handling (using `signal()`) for `SIGINT` (Ctrl+C) to ensure graceful shutdown. Upon receiving `SIGINT`, the program should close the log file, set the alarm LED LOW, and exit cleanly.

Ensure all functions are well-documented with comments explaining their purpose and parameters. The code should be easily understandable by a beginner C programmer. Avoid any deprecated functions or libraries. Assume the necessary wiringPi library is installed using `sudo apt-get install wiringpi`.
explanation: The C code interfaces with a Honeywell AWM5300V airflow sensor via an MCP3008 ADC on a Raspberry Pi. It reads the sensor data, calculates the voltage, logs it to a file, and triggers an alarm if the airflow (voltage) drops below a defined threshold. The code uses the wiringPi library for GPIO and SPI communication and includes signal handling for graceful shutdown.

Certainly! Below is a C program designed for a Raspberry Pi 3 Model B+ that interfaces with a Honeywell AWM5300V airflow sensor. The sensor provides an analog voltage output which we will read using ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter). We'll use the `wiringPi` library for GPIO operations and logging.

### Prerequisites:
1. Install `wiringPi` on your Raspberry Pi:
   ```sh
   sudo apt-get update
   git clone https://github.com/WiringPi/WiringPi.git
   cd WiringPi
   ./build
   ```

2. Ensure you have the necessary permissions to access the GPIO pins:
   ```sh
   sudo usermod -a -G gpio $USER
   ```

3. Run your programs as root or with `sudo`.

### Program Code:

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <sys/time.h>
#include <wiringPi.h>

// Constants
#define SENSOR_PIN 0    // Analog pin connected to the sensor
#define LOG_FILE "airflow_log.txt"
#define THRESHOLD_VOLTAGE 0.6 // Threshold voltage in volts (adjust based on your sensor)

// Function prototypes
int getVoltage(int pin);
void logData(double voltage, char* timestamp);
void setupGPIO();

int main() {
    int pin = SENSOR_PIN;
    double voltage;
    struct timeval tv;

    // Initialize wiringPi
    if (wiringPiSetup() == -1) {
        printf("wiringPi setup failed!\n");
        return 1;
    }

    setupGPIO();

    while (1) {
        // Get current time
        gettimeofday(&tv, NULL);
        // Read the voltage from the sensor
        voltage = getVoltage(pin);

        // Log the data
        logData(voltage, ctime((time_t*)&tv.tv_sec));

        // Check if the voltage is below the threshold
        if (voltage < THRESHOLD_VOLTAGE) {
            printf("Airflow is low: %f V\n", voltage);
            // Trigger an alarm (e.g., toggle a LED)
            pinMode(18, OUTPUT); // Assuming GPIO 18 is connected to an LED
            digitalWrite(18, HIGH);
            sleep(1); // Stay on for 1 second
            digitalWrite(18, LOW);
        }

        // Sleep for a short period before the next reading
        sleep(1);
    }

    return 0;
}

// Function to read the voltage from the ADC pin
int getVoltage(int pin) {
    int rawValue;
    float voltage;

    // Read the raw value from the ADC
    rawValue = analogRead(pin);
    // Convert raw value to voltage
    voltage = rawValue * (5.0 / 1023.0);

    return voltage;
}

// Function to log data to a file
void logData(double voltage, char* timestamp) {
    FILE *file;
    char logLine[100];

    // Open the log file
    file = fopen(LOG_FILE, "a");
    if (!file) {
        perror("Failed to open log file");
        exit(EXIT_FAILURE);
    }

    // Format the log line
    snprintf(logLine, sizeof(logLine), "%sVoltage: %.3f V\n", timestamp, voltage);

    // Write to the log file
    fprintf(file, logLine);
    fclose(file);
}

// Function to initialize GPIO
void setupGPIO() {
    // Set up GPIO for the LED (assuming GPIO 18 is used)
    pinMode(18, OUTPUT);
}
```

### Explanation:
1. **Constants**: Define constants for the sensor pin, log file name, and threshold voltage.
2. **Function Prototypes**: Declare functions to be used in the program.
3. **Main Function**:
   - Initializes `wiringPi`.
   - Sets up GPIO.
   - Continuously reads the sensor's analog value, logs it, and checks if it falls below the threshold.
4. **getVoltage**: Reads the analog value and converts it to a voltage.
5. **logData**: Logs the timestamp and voltage to a file.
6. **setupGPIO**: Configures the GPIO pin for the LED.

### Notes:
- Adjust the `THRESHOLD_VOLTAGE` based on the specifications of your Honeywell AWM5300V sensor.
- The program assumes the ADC input is connected to `pin 0` (WiringPi pin numbering).
- The LED is controlled by GPIO 18, which should be configured as an output.

Compile the program with:
```sh
gcc -o airflow_monitor airflow_monitor.c -lwiringPi
```

Run the compiled program with `sudo`:
```sh
sudo ./airflow_monitor
```

This program will continuously monitor the airflow and log the values to `airflow_log.txt`, and trigger an alarm when the airflow drops below the specified threshold.<|im_end|>

To develop a C application for Raspberry Pi 3 Model B+ that reads analog voltage from a Honeywell AWM5300V airflow sensor via an ADC (MCP3008 via SPI), logs voltage values to a file named `airflow_log.txt`, and activates an alarm on a GPIO pin if the voltage falls below a threshold, follow these steps:

1. **Hardware Setup:**
   - Connect the AWM5300V airflow sensor's output to a suitable analog input (e.g., GPIO 20).
   - Use an MCP3008 ADC connected to SPI0.
   - Set up the alarm GPIO (e.g., GPIO 17) to trigger an LED or other alert.

2. **Software Setup:**
   - Install wiringPi: `sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install wiringpi`
   - Create the C source file (e.g., `airflow_monitor.c`)

3. **C Code Implementation:**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <time.h>
#include <signal.h>
#include <pigpio.h>

// Define constants
#define SPI_CHANNEL 0
#define SPI_SPEED 1000000 // 1 MHz
#define ALARM_GPIO 17
#define LOG_FILE "airflow_log.txt"
#define VREF 3.3
#define THRESHOLD 0.5

// Global variables
int spi_handle;
FILE *log_file;
volatile sig_atomic_t terminate = 0;

// Function prototypes
int read_mcp3008(int channel);
float get_airflow_voltage(int adc_value);
void log_airflow_data(float voltage);
void activate_alarm(void);
void signal_handler(int signal);

// Function to read data from MCP3008 ADC
int read_mcp3008(int channel) {
    if (channel < 0 || channel > 7) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Invalid MCP3008 channel: %d\n", channel);
        return -1;
    }

    char tx_buffer[3];
    char rx_buffer[3];

    // Construct SPI message
    tx_buffer[0] = 0x01;                  // Start bit
    tx_buffer[1] = ((channel & 0x07) << 4) | 0x80; // Single-ended mode, channel select
    tx_buffer[2] = 0x00;

    spiXfer(spi_handle, tx_buffer, rx_buffer, 3);

    int adc_value = ((rx_buffer[1] & 0x0F) << 8) + rx_buffer[2];
    return adc_value;
}

// Function to convert ADC value to voltage
float get_airflow_voltage(int adc_value) {
    float voltage = ((float)adc_value / 1023.0) * VREF;
    return voltage;
}

// Function to log airflow data to a file
void log_airflow_data(float voltage) {
    time_t rawtime;
    struct tm *timeinfo;
    char timestamp[80];

    time(&rawtime);
    timeinfo = localtime(&rawtime);

    strftime(timestamp, sizeof(timestamp), "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S", timeinfo);

    fprintf(log_file, "%s, %.3f\n", timestamp, voltage);
    fflush(log_file); // Ensure data is written immediately
}

// Function to activate the alarm
void activate_alarm(void) {
    gpioWrite(ALARM_GPIO, 1); // Set GPIO high
    gpioDelay(1000);          // Wait for 1 second
    gpioWrite(ALARM_GPIO, 0); // Set GPIO low
}

// Signal handler for graceful shutdown
void signal_handler(int signal) {
    printf("Caught signal %d, terminating...\n", signal);
    terminate = 1;
}

int main() {
    // Initialize pigpio
    if (gpioInitialise() < 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "pigpio initialisation failed\n");
        return 1;
    }

    // Open log file
    log_file = fopen(LOG_FILE, "a");
    if (log_file == NULL) {
        perror("Error opening log file");
        gpioTerminate();
        return 1;
    }

    // Configure alarm GPIO as output
    gpioSetMode(ALARM_GPIO, PI_OUTPUT);

    // Open SPI channel
    spi_handle = spiOpen(SPI_CHANNEL, SPI_SPEED, 0); // Channel 0, 1MHz, default flags
    if (spi_handle < 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "SPI open failed\n");
        fclose(log_file);
        gpioTerminate();
        return 1;
    }

    // Register signal handlers
    signal(SIGINT, signal_handler);
    signal(SIGTERM, signal_handler);

    printf("Airflow monitoring started. Press Ctrl+C to exit.\n");

    // Main loop
    while (!terminate) {
        int adc_value = read_mcp3008(0); // Read from channel 0
        if (adc_value < 0) {
            fprintf(stderr, "Failed to read ADC value\n");
            continue; // Skip to the next iteration
        }

        float voltage = get_airflow_voltage(adc_value);
        log_airflow_data(voltage);

        if (voltage < THRESHOLD) {
            activate_alarm();
        }

        gpioDelay(100); // Sample every 100ms
    }

    // Cleanup
    printf("Cleaning up...\n");
    spiClose(spi_handle);
    fclose(log_file);
    gpioTerminate();

    return 0;
}
```

### Instructions:

1. **Compile the code:**
   ```bash
   gcc -o airflow_monitor airflow_monitor.c -lpigpio
   ```

2. **Run the program:**
   ```bash
   sudo ./airflow_monitor
   ```

3. **Monitor the `airflow_log.txt` file:**
   You can use `tail -f airflow_log.txt` to see the latest entries.

4. **Stop the program:**
   Press Ctrl+C or execute `sudo kill -SIGINT <process_id>`.

This implementation uses `wiringPi` via `pigpio` for SPI communication and GPIO control. Make sure to test the SPI connection and airflow sensor before deploying in a critical environment.<|im_end|>

#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <wiringPi.h>
#include <wiringPiSPI.h>
#include <time.h>
#include <signal.h>
#include <string.h>

// Constants
#define SPI_CHANNEL 0
#define SPI_SPEED 1000000 // 1 MHz
#define SENSOR_VOLTAGE_MIN 0.0
#define SENSOR_VOLTAGE_MAX 4.0
#define SENSOR_FLOW_MIN 0.0
#define SENSOR_FLOW_MAX 25.0
#define WARNING_PIN 18
#define FLOW_THRESHOLD 1.0
#define LOG_FILE "airflow_log.txt"
#define SAMPLE_INTERVAL_MS 100

// Global Variables (Minimize usage)
static volatile int keepRunning = 1;

// Function Prototypes
int read_adc(int channel);
float calculate_flow_rate(int adc_value);
void log_airflow(float flow_rate);
void warning_handler(void);
void signal_handler(int sig);

// Signal Handler
void signal_handler(int sig) {
    if (sig == SIGINT) {
        printf("Caught SIGINT, exiting gracefully...\n");
        keepRunning = 0;
    }
}

// Read ADC Value from MCP3008
int read_adc(int channel) {
    unsigned char buffer[3];
    int adc_value;

    buffer[0] = 0x01; // Start bit
    buffer[1] = ((channel & 0x07) << 4) | 0x80; // Single-ended mode, channel selection
    buffer[2] = 0x00;

    wiringPiSPIDataRW(SPI_CHANNEL, buffer, 3);

    adc_value = ((buffer[1] & 0x03) << 8) + buffer[2];

    return adc_value;
}

// Calculate Flow Rate from ADC Value
float calculate_flow_rate(int adc_value) {
    float voltage = (adc_value / 1023.0) * 5.0;
    float flow_rate = (voltage - SENSOR_VOLTAGE_MIN) * (SENSOR_FLOW_MAX - SENSOR_FLOW_MIN) / (SENSOR_VOLTAGE_MAX - SENSOR_VOLTAGE_MIN) + SENSOR_FLOW_MIN;
    return flow_rate;
}

// Log Airflow Data to File
void log_airflow(float flow_rate) {
    FILE *fp;
    time_t t = time(NULL);
    struct tm tm = *localtime(&t);
    char timestamp[64];

    // Format timestamp
    strftime(timestamp, sizeof(timestamp), "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S", &tm);

    fp = fopen(LOG_FILE, "a");
    if (fp == NULL) {
        perror("Error opening log file");
        return;
    }

    fprintf(fp, "%s: Flow Rate = %.2f SLPM\n", timestamp, flow_rate);
    fclose(fp);
}

// Warning Handler
void warning_handler(void) {
    digitalWrite(WARNING_PIN, HIGH); // Set warning pin high
    delay(1000);                       // Wait for 1 second
    digitalWrite(WARNING_PIN, LOW);  // Set warning pin low
}

int main(void) {
    int adc_value;
    float flow_rate;
    time_t t;

    // Initialize WiringPi
    if (wiringPiSetup() == -1) {
        fprintf(stderr, "WiringPi initialization failed!\n");
        return 1;
    }

    // Initialize SPI
    if (wiringPiSPISetup(SPI_CHANNEL, SPI_SPEED) == -1) {
        fprintf(stderr, "SPI initialization failed!\n");
        return 1;
    }

    // Set Warning Pin as Output
    pinMode(WARNING_PIN, OUTPUT);

    // Register Signal Handler for Ctrl+C
    signal(SIGINT, signal_handler);

    printf("Airflow Monitoring System started. Press Ctrl+C to exit.\n");

    while (keepRunning) {
        // Read ADC Value
        adc_value = read_adc(0);

        // Calculate Flow Rate
        flow_rate = calculate_flow_rate(adc_value);

        // Log Airflow Data
        log_airflow(flow_rate);

        // Check Threshold and Trigger Warning
        if (flow_rate < FLOW_THRESHOLD) {
            warning_handler();
        }

        delay(SAMPLE_INTERVAL_MS); // Sample every 100ms
    }

    printf("Exiting program.\n");
    return 0;
}<|im_end|>

task: Create a Buzzer Actuators Application for Aircraft Monitoring
category: Aircraft Proximity Alert System
input: Write a C program for Raspberry Pi 5 using the `pigpio` library to implement a rudimentary proximity alert system; drive two KY-012 active buzzer modules connected to GPIO pins 17 and 18, modulating their frequencies based on simulated distance readings from stdin (0-100cm), triggering distinct alarm patterns when readings fall below 20cm and 10cm, respectively, with graceful error handling for `pigpio` initialization and invalid input.
prompt:
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi 5 using the `pigpio` library to implement a multi-sensor proximity alert system for aircraft monitoring. Simulate multiple proximity sensors by reading distance readings from stdin, each representing a sensor aimed in a different direction (e.g., forward, left, right). Assume three virtual sensors, providing three distance readings separated by spaces on each line of stdin (e.g., "25 50 10"). Drive three KY-012 active buzzer modules connected to GPIO pins 17, 18, and 22, modulating their frequencies independently based on the corresponding sensor readings. Implement a functional programming style using functions to encapsulate tasks like reading input, processing sensor data, and controlling the buzzers.

Specifically:

1.  **Input:** Read three space-separated integer distance values (0-100cm) from stdin on each line, representing the readings from the three virtual proximity sensors.
2.  **Sensor Processing:** Create functions to process each sensor reading.
    *   If a sensor reading falls below 20cm, trigger a "warning" alarm pattern for the corresponding buzzer.
    *   If a sensor reading falls below 10cm, trigger a "critical" alarm pattern for the corresponding buzzer.
    *   If a sensor reading is above 20cm, the corresponding buzzer should be silent.
3.  **Alarm Patterns:** Implement distinct alarm patterns for "warning" and "critical" states using PWM frequency modulation on the buzzers. The "warning" pattern should be a slow, intermittent beep (e.g., 500Hz for 0.2 seconds, then off for 0.5 seconds). The "critical" pattern should be a fast, continuous beep (e.g., 1000Hz continuously).
4.  **Buzzer Control:** Use `pigpio` library functions (e.g., `gpioPWM`, `gpioWrite`) to control the buzzers connected to GPIO pins 17, 18, and 22. Create functions to encapsulate the buzzer control logic for each sensor.
5.  **Error Handling:** Implement robust error handling for `pigpio` initialization using `gpioInitialise()`. If initialization fails, print an error message to stderr and exit gracefully. Handle invalid input from stdin (e.g., non-numeric values, incorrect number of values) by printing an error message to stderr and continuing to the next line of input.
6.  **Functional Style:** Emphasize functional programming principles by using functions for distinct tasks (input reading, sensor processing, buzzer control). Minimize side effects and mutable state.
7.  **Graceful Shutdown:** Implement signal handling (e.g., using `signal(SIGINT, handler)`) to ensure a graceful shutdown when the program is interrupted (e.g., by pressing Ctrl+C). In the signal handler, stop all buzzers and terminate the `pigpio` library using `gpioTerminate()`.
8.  **Libraries:** Use only standard C libraries and the `pigpio` library. The `pigpio` library must be installed via `sudo apt-get install pigpio`. Start the `pigpiod` daemon before running the program using `sudo pigpiod`.
9.  **Compilation:** The code should be compilable using `gcc` with appropriate flags (e.g., `-lpigpio -pthread`).
10. **Example:** If the input is "25 50 10", buzzer 1 (GPIO 17) should be silent, buzzer 2 (GPIO 18) should be silent, and buzzer 3 (GPIO 22) should emit the "critical" alarm pattern.

The program should continuously read input from stdin, process the sensor data, and control the buzzers until it is interrupted. The complexity should be intermediate, showcasing good coding practices and effective use of the `pigpio` library. The application is designed for aircraft monitoring, where proximity to other objects is critical.
explanation: The code implements a multi-sensor proximity alert system using the pigpio library. It reads distance readings from stdin, processes them to determine alarm states, and controls buzzers connected to GPIO pins to indicate warnings or critical alerts. The `gpioSetPWMfrequency` function is used to set the PWM frequency before setting the duty cycle using `gpioPWM`.

To create a C program for a Raspberry Pi 5 that uses the `pigpio` library to control two KY-012 active buzzer modules, we need to follow these steps:

1. Initialize the `pigpio` library.
2. Set up the GPIO pins for the buzzers.
3. Read distance values from standard input.
4. Based on the distance, trigger different alarm patterns on the buzzers.
5. Handle errors for `pigpio` initialization and invalid inputs.

Here is the complete code for this task:

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <pigpio.h>

#define BUZZER_PIN_1 17
#define BUZZER_PIN_2 18

// Function prototypes
int initialize_pigpio(void);
void cleanup_pigpio(int status);
void play_alarm(double distance);

int main() {
    int status;

    // Initialize pigpio
    if ((status = initialize_pigpio()) != PI_OK) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Failed to initialize pigpio: %s\n", pigpio_error(status));
        return 1;
    }

    // Set up buzzer pins as PWM output
    gpioSetMode(BUZZER_PIN_1, PI_OUTPUT);
    gpioSetPWMfrequency(BUZZER_PIN_1, 440); // Set frequency to 440 Hz
    gpioPWM(BUZZER_PIN_1, 0); // Turn off buzzer

    gpioSetMode(BUZZER_PIN_2, PI_OUTPUT);
    gpioSetPWMfrequency(BUZZER_PIN_2, 440); // Set frequency to 440 Hz
    gpioPWM(BUZZER_PIN_2, 0); // Turn off buzzer

    while (1) {
        double distance;
        char line[100];

        // Read distance value from stdin
        printf("Enter distance in cm (0-100): ");
        if (!fgets(line, sizeof(line), stdin)) {
            fprintf(stderr, "Error reading input.\n");
            break;
        }

        if (sscanf(line, "%lf", &distance) != 1 || distance < 0 || distance > 100) {
            fprintf(stderr, "Invalid input! Please enter a number between 0 and 100.\n");
            continue;
        }

        // Play appropriate alarm based on distance
        play_alarm(distance);
    }

    // Cleanup
    cleanup_pigpio(0);

    return 0;
}

int initialize_pigpio(void) {
    return gpioInitialise();
}

void cleanup_pigpio(int status) {
    gpioTerminate();
    exit(status);
}

void play_alarm(double distance) {
    if (distance < 10.0) {
        // Critical low threshold
        gpioPWM(BUZZER_PIN_1, 1000); // High volume
        gpioPWM(BUZZER_PIN_2, 1000); // High volume
        usleep(500000); // 0.5 seconds
        gpioPWM(BUZZER_PIN_1, 0);
        gpioPWM(BUZZER_PIN_2, 0);
        usleep(500000); // 0.5 seconds
    } else if (distance < 20.0) {
        // Low threshold
        gpioPWM(BUZZER_PIN_1, 500); // Medium volume
        gpioPWM(BUZZER_PIN_2, 500); // Medium volume
        usleep(250000); // 0.25 seconds
        gpioPWM(BUZZER_PIN_1, 0);
        gpioPWM(BUZZER_PIN_2, 0);
        usleep(250000); // 0.25 seconds
    } else {
        // Normal operation
        gpioPWM(BUZZER_PIN_1, 0);
        gpioPWM(BUZZER_PIN_2, 0);
    }
}
```

### Explanation:
1. **Initialization and Cleanup**: The `initialize_pigpio` function initializes the `pigpio` library, and `cleanup_pigpio` terminates it.
2. **GPIO Setup**: The buzzer pins are set up as PWM outputs.
3. **Input Handling**: The program reads a distance value from the user and checks if it's within the valid range (0-100 cm).
4. **Alarm Patterns**: Different alarm patterns are played based on the distance:
   - Below 10 cm: High-volume continuous beeping.
   - Between 10 cm and 20 cm: Medium-volume intermittent beeping.
   - Above 20 cm: No sound (normal operation).

### Notes:
- Ensure you have the `pigpio` library installed on your Raspberry Pi 5.
- Run the program with root privileges or configure `pigpio` to allow non-root users.
- Adjust the buzzer frequencies and durations as needed for your specific application.<|im_end|>

#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <signal.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <pigpio.h>

// Define GPIO pins for the buzzers
#define BUZZER1_PIN 17
#define BUZZER2_PIN 18

// Function prototypes
int read_distance();
void generate_frequency(int frequency);
void generate_alarm1();
void generate_alarm2();
void cleanup(int signal);

// Global variables
volatile sig_atomic_t terminate = 0;

// Signal handler function
void cleanup(int signal) {
    printf("Cleaning up and exiting...\n");
    gpioTerminate();
    exit(0);
}

// Function to read distance from stdin
int read_distance() {
    int distance;
    char input[3];

    // Read input from stdin
    if (fgets(input, sizeof(input), stdin) == NULL) {
        return -1; // Indicate error (e.g., EOF)
    }

    // Parse the input as an integer
    if (sscanf(input, "%d", &distance) != 1) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Invalid input: %s\n", input);
        return -1; // Indicate parsing error
    }

    return distance;
}

// Function to generate a square wave at a specified frequency
void generate_frequency(int frequency) {
    gpioSetMode(BUZZER1_PIN, PI_OUTPUT);
    gpioSetMode(BUZZER2_PIN, PI_OUTPUT);

    double period = 1.0 / frequency;
    double half_period = period / 2.0;
    long int delay_us = (long int)(half_period * 1000000.0);

    while (!terminate) {
        gpioWrite(BUZZER1_PIN, 1);
        gpioDelay(delay_us);
        gpioWrite(BUZZER1_PIN, 0);
        gpioDelay(delay_us);

        gpioWrite(BUZZER2_PIN, 1);
        gpioDelay(delay_us);
        gpioWrite(BUZZER2_PIN, 0);
        gpioDelay(delay_us);
    }
}

// Function to generate alarm 1 (higher frequency)
void generate_alarm1() {
    generate_frequency(2000); // 2 kHz
}

// Function to generate alarm 2 (lower frequency)
void generate_alarm2() {
    generate_frequency(400);  // 400 Hz
}

int main() {
    // Initialize pigpio library
    if (gpioInitialise() < 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "pigpio initialisation failed\n");
        return 1;
    }

    // Set up signal handlers for graceful shutdown
    signal(SIGINT, cleanup);
    signal(SIGTERM, cleanup);

    printf("Proximity alert system started. Enter distance (0-100cm). Press Ctrl+C to exit.\n");

    while (!terminate) {
        int distance = read_distance();

        if (distance == -1) {
            // Handle input errors (EOF or parsing error)
            gpioTerminate();
            return 1;
        }

        if (distance >= 0 && distance <= 100) {
            if (distance < 20) {
                generate_alarm2();
            } else if (distance < 10) {
                generate_alarm1();
            } else {
                // Stop the alarms for distances greater than 10cm
                gpioWrite(BUZZER1_PIN, 0);
                gpioWrite(BUZZER2_PIN, 0);
            }
        } else {
            fprintf(stderr, "Distance out of range (0-100cm): %d\n", distance);
        }

        gpioDelay(500000); // Delay for 0.5 seconds
    }

    // Cleanup and exit
    gpioTerminate();
    return 0;
}<|im_end|>

Create a C code-based application for the Raspberry Pi 5 using the pigpio library that reads simulated distance values (ranging from 0 to 100 cm) from standard input. Depending on the distance, control two KY-012 active buzzers connected to GPIO17 and GPIO18 by adjusting their output patterns. #include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <signal.h>
#include <pigpio.h>

// Define GPIO pins for the buzzers
#define BUZZER1_PIN 17
#define BUZZER2_PIN 18

// Function prototypes
float read_distance();
void buzzer_pattern(int pin, float distance);
void signal_handler(int signal);

// Global variable to indicate program termination
volatile sig_atomic_t terminate = 0;

// Signal handler function
void signal_handler(int signal) {
    if (signal == SIGINT) {
        fprintf(stderr, "\nCaught SIGINT, exiting gracefully...\n");
        terminate = 1;
    }
}

// Function to read simulated distance from standard input
float read_distance() {
    char buffer[10];
    int bytes_read = 0;
    int i = 0;

    // Read up to 9 characters from stdin
    while ((bytes_read = getchar()) != EOF && bytes_read != '\n' && i < 9) {
        buffer[i] = (char)bytes_read;
        i++;
    }

    // Null-terminate the string
    buffer[i] = '\0';

    // Validate the input
    if (i > 0 && i <= 9 && buffer[0] >= '0' && buffer[0] <= '9') {
        return (float)atoi(buffer);
    } else {
        fprintf(stderr, "Invalid input. Please enter a number between 0 and 100.\n");
        return -1.0; // Indicate an error
    }
}

// Function to generate buzzer patterns based on distance
void buzzer_pattern(int pin, float distance) {
    if (distance < 20.0) {
        // Alert pattern: Short bursts of 500Hz, 50ms each, with 50ms pause between bursts
        gpioPWM(pin, 255); // Turn buzzer on
        gpioDelay(50000);   // 50ms delay
        gpioPWM(pin, 0);    // Turn buzzer off
        gpioDelay(50000);   // 50ms delay
    } else if (distance < 10.0) {
        // Urgent alarm pattern: Continuous 1000Hz tone for 1 second
        gpioPWM(pin, 255); // Turn buzzer on
        gpioDelay(1000000); // 1 second delay
        gpioPWM(pin, 0);    // Turn buzzer off
    }
}

int main() {
    // Register signal handler for Ctrl+C
    signal(SIGINT, signal_handler);

    // Initialize pigpio library
    if (gpioInitialise() < 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "pigpio initialisation failed\n");
        return 1;
    }

    // Set GPIO modes for buzzer pins as output
    gpioSetMode(BUZZER1_PIN, PI_OUTPUT);
    gpioSetMode(BUZZER2_PIN, PI_OUTPUT);

    // Main loop
    while (!terminate) {
        // Read distance from standard input
        float distance = read_distance();

        if (distance >= 0.0) {
            // Generate buzzer patterns based on distance
            buzzer_pattern(BUZZER1_PIN, distance);
            buzzer_pattern(BUZZER2_PIN, distance);
        }

        // Delay to avoid excessive CPU usage
        gpioDelay(10000); // 10ms delay
    }

    // Terminate pigpio library before exiting
    gpioTerminate();

    return 0;
}<|im_end|>

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <pigpio.h>

#define BUZZER_PIN_1 17
#define BUZZER_PIN_2 18

// Function prototypes
int initialize_pigpio(void);
void cleanup_pigpio(int status);
void play_alarm(double distance);

int main() {
    int status;

    // Initialize pigpio
    if ((status = initialize_pigpio()) != PI_OK) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Failed to initialize pigpio: %s\n", pigpio_error(status));
        return 1;
    }

    // Set up buzzer pins as PWM output
    gpioSetMode(BUZZER_PIN_1, PI_OUTPUT);
    gpioSetPWMfrequency(BUZZER_PIN_1, 440); // Set frequency to 440 Hz
    gpioPWM(BUZZER_PIN_1, 0); // Turn off buzzer

    gpioSetMode(BUZZER_PIN_2, PI_OUTPUT);
    gpioSetPWMfrequency(BUZZER_PIN_2, 440); // Set frequency to 440 Hz
    gpioPWM(BUZZER_PIN_2, 0); // Turn off buzzer

    while (1) {
        double distance;
        char line[100];

        // Read distance value from stdin
        printf("Enter distance in cm (0-100): ");
        if (!fgets(line, sizeof(line), stdin)) {
            fprintf(stderr, "Error reading input.\n");
            break;
        }

        if (sscanf(line, "%lf", &distance) != 1 || distance < 0 || distance > 100) {
            fprintf(stderr, "Invalid input! Please enter a number between 0 and 100.\n");
            continue;
        }

        // Play appropriate alarm based on distance
        play_alarm(distance);
    }

    // Cleanup
    cleanup_pigpio(0);

    return 0;
}

int initialize_pigpio(void) {
    return gpioInitialise();
}

void cleanup_pigpio(int status) {
    gpioTerminate();
    exit(status);
}

void play_alarm(double distance) {
    if (distance < 10.0) {
        // Critical low threshold
        gpioPWM(BUZZER_PIN_1, 1000); // High volume
        gpioPWM(BUZZER_PIN_2, 1000); // High volume
        usleep(500000); // 0.5 seconds
        gpioPWM(BUZZER_PIN_1, 0);
        gpioPWM(BUZZER_PIN_2, 0);
        usleep(500000); // 0.5 seconds
    } else if (distance < 20.0) {
        // Low threshold
        gpioPWM(BUZZER_PIN_1, 500); // Medium volume
        gpioPWM(BUZZER_PIN_2, 500); // Medium volume
        usleep(250000); // 0.25 seconds
        gpioPWM(BUZZER_PIN_1, 0);
        gpioPWM(BUZZER_PIN_2, 0);
        usleep(250000); // 0.25 seconds
    } else {
        // Normal operation
        gpioPWM(BUZZER_PIN_1, 0);
        gpioPWM(BUZZER_PIN_2, 0);
    }
}
```

// Define GPIO pins for the buzzers

#define BUZZER2_GPIO 18

// Global variable to indicate program termination
volatile sig_atomic_t program_running = 1;

// Function prototypes
int read_distance();
void set_buzzer_frequency(int gpio, int frequency);
void cleanup_gpio(void);
void signal_handler(int signal);

// Signal handler function
void signal_handler(int signal) {
    if (signal == SIGINT) {
        fprintf(stderr, "\nCaught SIGINT, exiting gracefully...\n");
        program_running = 0;
    }
}

// Function to read the simulated distance from stdin
int read_distance() {
    char buffer[3];
    memset(buffer, 0, sizeof(buffer)); // Clear the buffer

    printf("Enter distance (0-100): ");
    if (fgets(buffer, sizeof(buffer), stdin) == NULL) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Error reading from stdin.\n");
        return -1; // Indicate an error
    }

    // Remove trailing newline character
    size_t len = strlen(buffer);
    if (len > 0 && buffer[len - 1] == '\n') {
        buffer[len - 1] = '\0';
    }

    char *endptr;
    long distance = strtol(buffer, &endptr, 10);

    // Check for errors during conversion
    if (*endptr != '\0' || distance < 0 || distance > 100) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Invalid distance value: %s\n", buffer);
        return -1; // Indicate an error
    }

    return (int)distance;
}

// Function to set the frequency of the buzzer
void set_buzzer_frequency(int gpio, int frequency) {
    if (gpioWrite(gpio, 1) != 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Error setting buzzer HIGH for GPIO %d\n", gpio);
        return;
    }
    gpioSetPWMfrequency(gpio, frequency);
    gpioPWM(gpio, 128); // Duty cycle 50%
}

// Function to clean up GPIO pins before exiting
void cleanup_gpio(void) {
    gpioTerminate();
    printf("\nGPIO pins cleaned up and pigpio terminated.\n");
}

int main() {
    // Register the signal handler for SIGINT (Ctrl+C)
    if (signal(SIGINT, signal_handler) == SIG_ERR) {
        perror("signal");
        return 1;
    }

    // Initialize pigpio
    if (gpioInitialise() < 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "pigpio initialisation failed\n");
        return 1;
    }

    // Set GPIO pins as outputs
    if (gpioSetMode(BUZZER1_GPIO, PI_OUTPUT) != 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Failed to set GPIO %d as output\n", BUZZER1_GPIO);
        cleanup_gpio();
        return 1;
    }
    if (gpioSetMode(BUZZER2_GPIO, PI_OUTPUT) != 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Failed to set GPIO %d as output\n", BUZZER2_GPIO);
        cleanup_gpio();
        return 1;
    }

    // Main loop
    while (program_running) {
        int distance = read_distance();

        if (distance == -1) {
            // Error reading distance, skip this iteration
            continue;
        }

        // Determine which buzzer(s) to activate based on distance
        if (distance < 20) {
            // Both buzzers active
            set_buzzer_frequency(BUZZER1_GPIO, 440); // Frequency of buzzer1
            set_buzzer_frequency(BUZZER2_GPIO, 880); // Frequency of buzzer2
        } else if (distance < 10) {

            set_buzzer_frequency(BUZZER1_GPIO, 660); // Frequency of buzzer1
            gpioWrite(BUZZER2_GPIO, 0); // Turn off buzzer2
        } else {
            // No buzzers active
            gpioWrite(BUZZER1_GPIO, 0);
            gpioWrite(BUZZER2_GPIO, 0);
        }

        // Small delay to prevent excessive CPU usage
        usleep(50000); // 50ms
    }

    // Cleanup GPIO and terminate pigpio before exiting
    cleanup_gpio();

    return 0;
}<|im_end|>

4.  **Door Lock Control Logic:**

    *   Motion detected by the PIR sensor should *only* unlock both door locks.
    *   Each door lock should be activated for a duration of 3 seconds. After 3 seconds, the lock should automatically deactivate. Use `pigpio`'s `gpioDelay()` function for timing.

5.  **Logging:** Create a log file named `lock_events.log`. Each lock event (button press, motion detected, lock activation, lock deactivation) should be logged with a timestamp, lock ID (17 or 27), and action (e.g., "Button Press", "Motion Detected", "Lock 17 Activated", "Lock 27 Deactivated"). Use standard C library functions like `fopen()`, `fprintf()`, `time()`, and `localtime()` for file I/O and timestamp generation.

6.  **Error Handling:** Implement robust error handling. Check the return values of `gpioInitialise()` and `gpioSetMode()` and log any errors to `lock_events.log` and `stderr`. Include checks for file operations (e.g., `fopen()`). If `gpioInitialise()` fails, the program should exit gracefully with an error message.

7.  **Functional Programming Style:** Structure the code into small, reusable functions. For example:
    *   `initGPIO()`: Initializes all GPIO pins.

8.  **Signal Handling:** Implement signal handling for `SIGINT` (Ctrl+C) to ensure that `gpioTerminate()` is called before the program exits, releasing the GPIO resources. Use `signal()` for signal handling.

9.  **Compilation:** The program should be compilable using `gcc` with the `-lpigpio` flag (e.g., `gcc -o door_control door_control.c -lpigpio -pthread`).

To create a C program for the Raspberry Pi Zero W that uses the `pigpio` library to control two 12V electromagnetic door locks, we need to follow these steps:

1. Initialize the GPIO pins.
2. Set up the push button for debouncing.
3. Control the door locks based on the button press.
4. Log the events to a file.

First, make sure you have the `pigpio` library installed. You can install it via `apt-get`:

```sh
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install pigpiod
```

Then, you can write the C program as follows:

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <pigpio.h>
#include <time.h>

#define BUTTON_PIN 4
#define LOCK1_PIN 17
#define LOCK2_PIN 27

#define DEBOUNCE_TIME 200 // 200 microseconds

void log_event(int lock_id, char *action) {
    FILE *fp;
    time_t t;
    struct tm *tm_info;
    char buffer[80];

    time(&t);
    tm_info = localtime(&t);

    strftime(buffer, 80, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S", tm_info);

    fp = fopen("lock_events.log", "a");
    if (fp == NULL) {
        perror("Failed to open lock_events.log");
        exit(EXIT_FAILURE);
    }

    fprintf(fp, "%s, Lock %d, %s\n", buffer, lock_id, action);
    fclose(fp);
}

int main(void) {
    int button_state, lock1_state, lock2_state;
    uint32_t start_time;

    if (gpioInitialise() < 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "pigpio initialization failed.\n");
        return -1;
    }

    gpioSetMode(BUTTON_PIN, PI_INPUT);
    gpioSetPullUpDown(BUTTON_PIN, PI_PUD_UP);
    gpioSetMode(LOCK1_PIN, PI_OUTPUT);
    gpioSetMode(LOCK2_PIN, PI_OUTPUT);

    while (1) {
        button_state = gpioRead(BUTTON_PIN);
        if (button_state == 0) { // Button is pressed
            start_time = micros();
            do {
                button_state = gpioRead(BUTTON_PIN);
                sleep(1); // Debounce delay
            } while (button_state == 0 && micros() - start_time < DEBOUNCE_TIME);

            if (button_state == 0) { // Button is still pressed after debounce
                // Toggle the state of the locks
                lock1_state = !gpioRead(LOCK1_PIN);
                lock2_state = !gpioRead(LOCK2_PIN);

                gpioWrite(LOCK1_PIN, lock1_state);
                gpioWrite(LOCK2_PIN, lock2_state);

                log_event(lock1_state ? 1 : 2, lock1_state ? "Unlock" : "Lock");
                log_event(lock2_state ? 1 : 2, lock2_state ? "Unlock" : "Lock");
            }
        }
    }

    gpioTerminate();

    return 0;
}
```

### Explanation:
1. **Initialization**: The program initializes the GPIO pins for the button and the locks. It sets up pull-up resistors on the button pin to ensure a clean reading when the button is not pressed.
2. **Debouncing**: The program checks the button state multiple times with a short delay to debounce the input. If the button is held down for more than the debounce time, it toggles the state of the locks.
3. **Logging**: The program logs the event to `lock_events.log`, including the timestamp, lock ID, and action taken (lock or unlock).
4. **Main Loop**: The program runs in an infinite loop, continuously monitoring the button state.

### Building and Running:
To compile the program, use the following command:

```sh
gcc -o door_lock_control door_lock_control.c -lpigpio
```

Then, run the compiled binary:

```sh
./door_lock_control
```

This program will keep running until you manually stop it. Make sure your locks are properly connected to the specified GPIO pins and that the `pigpiod` daemon is running.<|im_end|>

#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <time.h>
#include <signal.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <pigpio.h>

// Define GPIO pins
#define BUTTON_GPIO 4
#define LOCK1_GPIO 17
#define LOCK2_GPIO 27

// Define debounce time in milliseconds
#define DEBOUNCE_TIME 50

// Define log file path
#define LOG_FILE "lock_events.log"

// Structure to hold lock state and last activation time
typedef struct {
    int gpio;
    time_t last_activation;
    int activated;
} LockState;

// Global variables
LockState locks[2];
FILE *log_file = NULL;
int running = 1; // Flag to control the main loop

// Function prototypes
void setup_gpio();
void cleanup_gpio();
void button_interrupt_handler(int gpio, int level, uint32_t tick);
void activate_lock(int lock_id);
void deactivate_lock(int lock_id);
void log_event(const char *lock_id, const char *action);
char* get_timestamp();

// Signal handler function
void signal_handler(int signal)
{
    if (signal == SIGINT)
    {
        printf("Caught SIGINT, exiting...\n");
        running = 0;
    }
}

int main()
{
    // Initialize pigpio library
    if (gpioInitialise() < 0)
    {
        fprintf(stderr, "pigpio initialisation failed\n");
        return 1;
    }

    // Set up signal handler for Ctrl+C
    signal(SIGINT, signal_handler);

    // Initialize button interrupt
    if (gpioSetMode(BUTTON_GPIO, PI_INPUT) != 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Failed to set GPIO %d as input\n", BUTTON_GPIO);
        cleanup_gpio();
        return 1;
    }

    if (gpioSetPullUpDown(BUTTON_GPIO, PI_PUD_UP) != 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Failed to set pull-up resistor on GPIO %d\n", BUTTON_GPIO);
        cleanup_gpio();
        return 1;
    }

    if (gpioSetISRFuncEx(BUTTON_GPIO, FALLING_EDGE, 0, button_interrupt_handler, NULL) != 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Failed to set ISR function on GPIO %d\n", BUTTON_GPIO);
        cleanup_gpio();
        return 1;
    }

    // Initialize locks
    locks[0].gpio = LOCK1_GPIO;
    locks[0].activated = 0;
    locks[0].last_activation = 0;

    locks[1].gpio = LOCK2_GPIO;
    locks[1].activated = 0;
    locks[1].last_activation = 0;

    // Open log file
    log_file = fopen(LOG_FILE, "a");
    if (log_file == NULL)
    {
        fprintf(stderr, "Failed to open log file %s\n", LOG_FILE);
        cleanup_gpio();
        return 1;
    }

    // Main loop
    while (running)
    {
        sleep(1); // Sleep for 1 second
    }

    // Cleanup before exiting
    cleanup_gpio();
    if (log_file != NULL)
    {
        fclose(log_file);
    }

    return 0;
}

// Button interrupt handler function
void button_interrupt_handler(int gpio, int level, uint32_t tick)
{
    if (level == 0) // Button pressed (LOW)
    {
        time_t now = time(NULL);
        time_t diff = difftime(now, locks[gpio - 1].last_activation);

        if (diff > (DEBOUNCE_TIME / 1000.0))
        {
            int lock_id = locks[gpio - 1].gpio;
            if (!locks[gpio - 1].activated)
            {
                activate_lock(lock_id);
                locks[gpio - 1].activated = 1;
                locks[gpio - 1].last_activation = now;
            } else {
                deactivate_lock(lock_id);
                locks[gpio - 1].activated = 0;
                locks[gpio - 1].last_activation = now;
            }
        }
    }
}

// Function to activate a lock
void activate_lock(int lock_id)
{
    printf("Activating lock on GPIO %d\n", lock_id);
    gpioWrite(lock_id, 1); // Assuming HIGH activates the lock
    log_event(get_timestamp(), "Activated");
}

// Function to deactivate a lock
void deactivate_lock(int lock_id)
{
    printf("Deactivating lock on GPIO %d\n", lock_id);
    gpioWrite(lock_id, 0); // Assuming LOW deactivates the lock
    log_event(get_timestamp(), "Deactivated");
}

// Function to log an event to the log file
void log_event(const char *lock_id, const char *action)
{
    char *timestamp = get_timestamp();
    if (timestamp != NULL)
    {
        fprintf(log_file, "%s,%s,%s\n", timestamp, lock_id, action);
        fflush(log_file); // Ensure data is written immediately
        free(timestamp);
    } else {
        fprintf(stderr, "Failed to get timestamp, cannot log event\n");
    }
}

// Function to get the current timestamp
char* get_timestamp()
{
    time_t rawtime;
    struct tm *timeinfo;
    char *timestamp = (char*)malloc(20 * sizeof(char)); // Allocate memory for the timestamp string

    if (timestamp == NULL) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Failed to allocate memory for timestamp\n");
        return NULL;
    }

    time(&rawtime);
    timeinfo = localtime(&rawtime);

    strftime(timestamp, 20, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S", timeinfo);
    return timestamp;
}

// Function to clean up GPIO resources
void cleanup_gpio()
{
    // Deactivate any active locks
    if (locks[0].activated) {
        deactivate_lock(LOCK1_GPIO);
    }
    if (locks[1].activated) {
        deactivate_lock(LOCK2_GPIO);
    }

    // Release GPIO pins
    gpioSetMode(LOCK1_GPIO, PI_OUTPUT);
    gpioSetMode(LOCK2_GPIO, PI_OUTPUT);

    // Terminate pigpio library
    gpioTerminate();
}<|im_end|>

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi Zero W using the `pigpio` library to control two 12V electromagnetic door locks (connected to GPIO 17 and 27), triggered by a single momentary push button connected to GPIO 4 and a Passive Infrared (PIR) motion sensor connected to GPIO 22. Implement debouncing for the push button using `pigpio`'s callback features. The program should adopt a functional programming style, suitable for a beginner-level understanding, and designed for an interactive art installation.

Specifically:

1.  **GPIO Control:** Use `pigpio` functions (e.g., `gpioInitialise()`, `gpioSetMode()`, `gpioWrite()`, `gpioSetAlertFuncEx()`) to manage the GPIO pins. Initialize GPIO 17 and 27 as outputs for the door locks, GPIO 4 as an input with a pull-up resistor for the push button, and GPIO 22 as an input for the PIR sensor.

2.  **Button Debouncing:** Implement debouncing for the push button using `gpioSetAlertFuncEx()` on GPIO 4. This function should call a separate function when the button state changes, which handles the logic for triggering the door locks.

3.  **Motion Sensor Integration:** Monitor GPIO 22 (PIR sensor) using `gpioSetAlertFuncEx()`. When motion is detected (GPIO 22 goes HIGH), trigger a separate function to log the event.

4.  **Door Lock Control Logic:**
    *   A press of the button should toggle the state of *both* door locks.
    *   Motion detected by the PIR sensor should *only* unlock both door locks.
    *   Each door lock should be activated for a duration of 3 seconds. After 3 seconds, the lock should automatically deactivate. Use `pigpio`'s `gpioDelay()` function for timing.

5.  **Logging:** Create a log file named `lock_events.log`. Each lock event (button press, motion detected, lock activation, lock deactivation) should be logged with a timestamp, lock ID (17 or 27), and action (e.g., "Button Press", "Motion Detected", "Lock 17 Activated", "Lock 27 Deactivated"). Use standard C library functions like `fopen()`, `fprintf()`, `time()`, and `localtime()` for file I/O and timestamp generation.

6.  **Error Handling:** Implement robust error handling. Check the return values of `gpioInitialise()` and `gpioSetMode()` and log any errors to `lock_events.log` and `stderr`. Include checks for file operations (e.g., `fopen()`). If `gpioInitialise()` fails, the program should exit gracefully with an error message.

7.  **Functional Programming Style:** Structure the code into small, reusable functions. For example:
    *   `initGPIO()`: Initializes all GPIO pins.
    *   `toggleLocks()`: Toggles the state of both door locks.
    *   `activateLock(int gpio, int duration_ms)`: Activates a specific door lock for a given duration.
    *   `logEvent(int gpio, const char *action)`: Logs an event to `lock_events.log`.
    *   `buttonCallback(int gpio, int level, uint32_t tick)`: Handles the button press event.
    *   `motionCallback(int gpio, int level, uint32_t tick)`: Handles the motion detection event.

8.  **Signal Handling:** Implement signal handling for `SIGINT` (Ctrl+C) to ensure that `gpioTerminate()` is called before the program exits, releasing the GPIO resources. Use `signal()` for signal handling.

9.  **Compilation:** The program should be compilable using `gcc` with the `-lpigpio` flag (e.g., `gcc -o door_control door_control.c -lpigpio -pthread`).

10. **Libraries:** Strictly use `pigpio`, `stdio.h`, `stdlib.h`, `time.h`, and `signal.h`. Do not use any C++ libraries or non-standard/proprietary libraries.

11. **Adherence to Standards:** Ensure all function calls and library usage are compatible with Raspberry Pi OS (Bookworm/Bullseye) and avoid deprecated features.

12. **Context:** Remember this is for an interactive art installation, so consider the user experience (e.g., clear logging, reliable operation).
explanation: The code initializes GPIO pins for door locks, a button, and a PIR sensor using the `pigpio` library. It implements debouncing for the button and uses callbacks for button presses and motion detection.  Lock events are logged to a file, and signal handling ensures proper cleanup upon program termination.

To create a C program for the Raspberry Pi Zero W that uses the `pigpio` library to control two 12V electromagnetic door locks, we need to follow these steps:

1. Initialize the GPIO pins.
2. Set up the push button for debouncing.
3. Control the door locks based on the button press.
4. Log the events to a file.

First, make sure you have the `pigpio` library installed. You can install it via `apt-get`:

```sh
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install pigpiod
```

Then, you can write the C program as follows:

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <pigpio.h>
#include <time.h>

#define BUTTON_PIN 4
#define LOCK1_PIN 17
#define LOCK2_PIN 27

#define DEBOUNCE_TIME 200 // 200 microseconds

void log_event(int lock_id, char *action) {
    FILE *fp;
    time_t t;
    struct tm *tm_info;
    char buffer[80];

    time(&t);
    tm_info = localtime(&t);

    strftime(buffer, 80, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S", tm_info);

    fp = fopen("lock_events.log", "a");
    if (fp == NULL) {
        perror("Failed to open lock_events.log");
        exit(EXIT_FAILURE);
    }

    fprintf(fp, "%s, Lock %d, %s\n", buffer, lock_id, action);
    fclose(fp);
}

int main(void) {
    int button_state, lock1_state, lock2_state;
    uint32_t start_time;

    if (gpioInitialise() < 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "pigpio initialization failed.\n");
        return -1;
    }

    gpioSetMode(BUTTON_PIN, PI_INPUT);
    gpioSetPullUpDown(BUTTON_PIN, PI_PUD_UP);
    gpioSetMode(LOCK1_PIN, PI_OUTPUT);
    gpioSetMode(LOCK2_PIN, PI_OUTPUT);

    while (1) {
        button_state = gpioRead(BUTTON_PIN);
        if (button_state == 0) { // Button is pressed
            start_time = micros();
            do {
                button_state = gpioRead(BUTTON_PIN);
                sleep(1); // Debounce delay
            } while (button_state == 0 && micros() - start_time < DEBOUNCE_TIME);

            if (button_state == 0) { // Button is still pressed after debounce
                // Toggle the state of the locks
                lock1_state = !gpioRead(LOCK1_PIN);
                lock2_state = !gpioRead(LOCK2_PIN);

                gpioWrite(LOCK1_PIN, lock1_state);
                gpioWrite(LOCK2_PIN, lock2_state);

                log_event(lock1_state ? 1 : 2, lock1_state ? "Unlock" : "Lock");
                log_event(lock2_state ? 1 : 2, lock2_state ? "Unlock" : "Lock");
            }
        }
    }

    gpioTerminate();

    return 0;
}
```

### Explanation:
1. **Initialization**: The program initializes the GPIO pins for the button and the locks. It sets up pull-up resistors on the button pin to ensure a clean reading when the button is not pressed.
2. **Debouncing**: The program checks the button state multiple times with a short delay to debounce the input. If the button is held down for more than the debounce time, it toggles the state of the locks.
3. **Logging**: The program logs the event to `lock_events.log`, including the timestamp, lock ID, and action taken (lock or unlock).
4. **Main Loop**: The program runs in an infinite loop, continuously monitoring the button state.

### Building and Running:
To compile the program, use the following command:

```sh
gcc -o door_lock_control door_lock_control.c -lpigpio
```

Then, run the compiled binary:

```sh
./door_lock_control
```

This program will keep running until you manually stop it. Make sure your locks are properly connected to the specified GPIO pins and that the `pigpiod` daemon is running.<|im_end|>

Create a C program for Raspberry Pi Zero W using the pigpio library that toggles two 12V electromagnetic door locks connected to GPIO pins 17 and 27. A momentary push button on GPIO 4 should control both locks, using software debouncing to prevent false triggers. Every lock activation or release should be logged with a timestamp, lock ID, and action (locked/unlocked) to a file named lock_events.log. Ensure proper error checks during GPIO and pigpio setup for robust operation.
<|im_end|>

#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <time.h>
#include <signal.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <pigpio.h>

// Define GPIO pins
#define BUTTON_PIN 4
#define LOCK1_PIN 17
#define LOCK2_PIN 27

// Define lock states
typedef enum {
    UNLOCKED,
    LOCKED
} LockState;

// Global variables
volatile int button_state = 0; // Button state: 1 = pressed, 0 = released
volatile int button_pressed = 0; // Flag to indicate button press
int lock1_state = UNLOCKED;
int lock2_state = UNLOCKED;
FILE *log_file = NULL;

// Function prototypes
void debounce_callback(int gpio, int level, uint32_t tick);
void log_event(LockState lock1_state, LockState lock2_state);
void unlock_locks();
void lock_locks();
void cleanup_handler(int signal);

// Debounce callback function
void debounce_callback(int gpio, int level, uint32_t tick) {
    if (gpio == BUTTON_PIN && level == 1) { // Button pressed
        button_pressed = 1;
        gpioDelay(50); // Small delay to ensure stable input
    } else if (gpio == BUTTON_PIN && level == 0) {
        button_pressed = 0;
    }
}

// Log event to file
void log_event(LockState lock1_state, LockState lock2_state) {
    time_t now = time(NULL);
    struct tm tstruct;
    char buf[80];
    localtime_r(&now, &tstruct);
    strftime(buf, sizeof(buf), "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S", &tstruct);

    if (log_file != NULL) {
        fprintf(log_file, "%s - Lock1: %s, Lock2: %s\n", buf, (lock1_state == LOCKED) ? "Locked" : "Unlocked",
                (lock2_state == LOCKED) ? "Locked" : "Unlocked");
        fflush(log_file); // Flush buffer to ensure immediate write
    } else {
        fprintf(stderr, "Error: Could not open log file.\n");
    }
}

// Unlock both locks
void unlock_locks() {
    gpioWrite(LOCK1_PIN, 1); // HIGH = unlock
    gpioWrite(LOCK2_PIN, 1);
    lock1_state = UNLOCKED;
    lock2_state = UNLOCKED;
    log_event(lock1_state, lock2_state);
    printf("Both locks unlocked.\n");
}

// Lock both locks
void lock_locks() {
    gpioWrite(LOCK1_PIN, 0); // LOW = lock
    gpioWrite(LOCK2_PIN, 0);
    lock1_state = LOCKED;
    lock2_state = LOCKED;
    log_event(lock1_state, lock2_state);
    printf("Both locks locked.\n");
}

// Cleanup handler for graceful exit
void cleanup_handler(int signal) {
    printf("\nCaught signal %d, cleaning up...\n", signal);
    unlock_locks(); // Ensure locks are unlocked before exiting
    if (log_file != NULL) {
        fclose(log_file);
    }
    gpioTerminate(); // Terminate pigpio
    exit(0);
}

int main() {
    // Initialize pigpio
    if (gpioInitialise() < 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "pigpio initialisation failed\n");
        return 1;
    }

    // Set GPIO modes
    gpioSetMode(BUTTON_PIN, PI_INPUT);
    gpioSetMode(LOCK1_PIN, PI_OUTPUT);
    gpioSetMode(LOCK2_PIN, PI_OUTPUT);

    // Set pull-up resistor for button pin
    gpioSetPullUpDown(BUTTON_PIN, PI_PUD_UP);

    // Open log file
    log_file = fopen("lock_events.log", "a");
    if (log_file == NULL) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Error opening log file!\n");
        gpioTerminate();
        return 1;
    }

    // Set up signal handler for Ctrl+C
    signal(SIGINT, cleanup_handler);

    // Set up interrupt for button press
    gpioSetAlertFuncEx(BUTTON_PIN, debounce_callback, NULL);

    printf("Door lock system started. Press Ctrl+C to exit.\n");

    while (1) {
        if (button_pressed) {
            button_pressed = 0; // Reset the flag after processing
            if (lock1_state == UNLOCKED) {
                lock_locks();
            } else {
                unlock_locks();
            }
        }
        gpioDelay(10); // Reduce CPU usage
    }

    return 0; // This line will never be reached due to the infinite loop
}<|im_end|>

#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <pigpio.h>
#include <time.h>
#include <string.h>

// Define GPIO pins
#define BUTTON_PIN   4
#define LOCK1_PIN    17
#define LOCK2_PIN    27

// Debounce time in milliseconds
#define DEBOUNCE_TIME 50

// File to log events
#define LOG_FILE "lock_events.log"

// Function prototypes
int read_button(int gpio);
void lock_unlock(int gpio, int lock_state);
void log_event(const char *event_message);
int initialize_gpio();
void cleanup_gpio();

int main() {
    // Initialize pigpio
    if (initialize_gpio() != 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "GPIO initialization failed. Exiting.\n");
        return 1;
    }

    printf("GPIO initialized successfully. Starting main loop.\n");

    int button_state = 0; // 0: unlocked, 1: locked
    time_t last_button_press = 0;

    // Main loop
    while (1) {
        int current_button_state = read_button(BUTTON_PIN);

        if (current_button_state == 1) {
            // Button is pressed
            time_t now = time(NULL);
            double seconds_since_press = difftime(now, last_button_press);

            if (seconds_since_press > (DEBOUNCE_TIME / 1000.0)) {
                // Debounced button press detected
                button_state = !button_state; // Toggle lock state

                // Lock/unlock the selected lock
                if (button_state) {
                    lock_unlock(LOCK1_PIN, 1); // Lock lock 1
                    log_event("Lock 1 locked.");
                } else {
                    lock_unlock(LOCK1_PIN, 0); // Unlock lock 1
                    log_event("Lock 1 unlocked.");
                }

                // Prepare to detect the next button press
                last_button_press = now;
            }
        }

        gpioDelay(10); // Small delay to prevent busy-waiting
    }

    // Cleanup GPIO before exiting (this part will not be reached in this example)
    cleanup_gpio();

    return 0;
}

// Function to read the button state with debouncing
int read_button(int gpio) {
    static int last_button_state = 0;
    static volatile int last_button_edge = 0; // Using volatile to avoid compiler optimizations
    static unsigned long last_debounce_time = 0;

    int current_button_state = gpioRead(gpio);

    if (current_button_state != last_button_state) {
        last_debounce_time = gpioTick();

        if ((gpioTick() - last_debounce_time) > (DEBOUNCE_TIME * 1000)) { // Convert ms to microseconds
            last_button_state = current_button_state;
            if (last_button_state == 1) {
                last_button_edge = 1; // Rising edge
            } else {
                last_button_edge = 0; // Falling edge
            }
        } else {
            if (last_button_state == 1) {
                last_button_edge = 0; // Ignore rising edge
            } else {
                last_button_edge = 1; // Ignore falling edge
            }
        }
    } else {
        if (last_button_state == 1 && last_button_edge == 1) {
            last_button_edge = 0; // Reset edge flag when stable
            return 1; // Button is pressed
        } else if (last_button_state == 0 && last_button_edge == 0) {
            last_button_edge = 1; // Reset edge flag when stable
            return 0; // Button is released
        }
    }

    return 0; // Button is not pressed
}

// Function to lock or unlock the specified door lock
void lock_unlock(int gpio, int lock_state) {
    if (gpioWrite(gpio, lock_state) != 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Error writing to GPIO %d\n", gpio);
    }
}

// Function to log events to a file
void log_event(const char *event_message) {
    FILE *fp = fopen(LOG_FILE, "a");
    if (fp == NULL) {
        perror("Error opening log file");
        return;
    }

    time_t t = time(NULL);
    struct tm tm = *localtime(&t);
    fprintf(fp, "%d-%02d-%02d %02d:%02d:%02d: %s\n", tm.tm_year + 1900, tm.tm_mon + 1, tm.tm_mday, tm.tm_hour, tm.tm_min, tm.tm_sec, event_message);

    fclose(fp);
}

// Function to initialize GPIO pins
int initialize_gpio() {
    // Initialize pigpio
    if (gpioInitialise() < 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "pigpio initialisation failed\n");
        return -1;
    }

    // Set button pin as input with pull-up resistor
    gpioSetMode(BUTTON_PIN, PI_INPUT);
    gpioSetPullUpDown(BUTTON_PIN, PI_PUD_UP);

    // Set lock pins as output
    gpioSetMode(LOCK1_PIN, PI_OUTPUT);
    gpioSetMode(LOCK2_PIN, PI_OUTPUT);

    // Initialize lock states to unlocked (0)
    gpioWrite(LOCK1_PIN, 0);
    gpioWrite(LOCK2_PIN, 0);

    return 0;
}

// Function to cleanup GPIO pins
void cleanup_gpio() {
    // Release GPIO pins
    gpioTerminate();
}<|im_end|>
